part_subtitle: Morphological Characteristics, Pupation Sites, Developmental Duration, Sensitivity, and Applied Importance

items.text: Chapter Six
Morphological Description of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

The pupa represents the transitional stage between the larva and the adult insect in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is a non-feeding stage; the pupa does not mine leaves, stems, or fruits and causes no direct damage to the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The [[term:pupation]]pupa[[/term]] represents the transitional stage between the larva and the adult insect in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is a non-feeding stage; the pupa does not mine leaves, stems, or fruits and causes no direct damage to the plant.

items.text: However, the absence of feeding does not mean that this stage is economically unimportant. Every viable pupa is capable of producing an adult moth that emerges from the pupation site, mates, lays eggs, and initiates a new pest generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the absence of feeding does not mean that this stage is economically unimportant. Every viable pupa is capable of producing an adult moth that emerges from the pupation site, mates, lays eggs, and initiates a new pest generation.

items.text: The importance of the pupal stage lies in the fact that it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The importance of the pupal stage lies in the fact that it:

items: Represents the transition from the damaging stage to the reproductive and dispersal stage.
Is often located in protected sites that are not easily reached by foliar spray treatments.
Can survive in soil or crop residues after the crop has ended.
May be transported with crates, packages, and sorting residues.
Helps the pest persist between cropping seasons.
May contribute to pest survival during cold periods.
Represents a target for cultural practices directed at soil and crop residues.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Represents the transition from the damaging stage to the reproductive and dispersal stage.
[[prepared]]Is often located in protected sites that are not easily reached by foliar spray treatments.
[[prepared]]Can survive in soil or crop residues after the crop has ended.
[[prepared]]May be transported with crates, packages, and sorting residues.
[[prepared]]Helps the pest persist between cropping seasons.
[[prepared]]May contribute to pest survival during cold periods.
[[prepared]]Represents a target for cultural practices directed at soil and crop residues.

items.text: Therefore, management of Tuta absoluta is not completed by controlling eggs and larvae alone, but must also include reducing pupal numbers and preventing adult moth emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore, management of Tuta absoluta is not completed by controlling eggs and larvae alone, but must also include reducing pupal numbers and preventing adult moth emergence.

items.text: Prepupal Stage
A fully grown larva does not transform immediately into a pupa, but first passes through a short transitional stage known as the:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A fully grown larva does not transform immediately into a pupa, but first passes through a short transitional stage known as the:

items.text: Prepupal Stage
This stage begins at the end of the fourth larval instar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This stage begins at the end of the fourth [[term:larval-instar]]larval instar[[/term]].

items.text: External Signs of the Prepupal Stage
As the larva approaches pupation, several clear changes appear, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]As the larva approaches pupation, several clear changes appear, including:

items: The larva leaves the mine or fruit in most cases.
Color changes from green to light green or pink.
Part of the digestive-tract contents is evacuated.
Movement increases while searching for a suitable site.
The larva hides in a folded leaf, crevice, or surface soil.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The larva leaves the mine or fruit in most cases.
[[prepared]]Color changes from green to light green or pink.
[[prepared]]Part of the digestive-tract contents is evacuated.
[[prepared]]Movement increases while searching for a suitable site.
[[prepared]]The larva hides in a folded leaf, crevice, or surface soil.

items.text: At this stage, the larva is less closely associated with the feeding site, and it may therefore be found far from the mine it created.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At this stage, the larva is less closely associated with the feeding site, and it may therefore be found far from the mine it created.

items.text: Searching for a Pupation Site
The fully grown larva leaves the feeding site and moves to a location that provides:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fully grown larva leaves the feeding site and moves to a location that provides:

items: A surface on which the cocoon can be secured.
Reduced risk of flooding or mechanical disturbance.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A surface on which the cocoon can be secured.
[[prepared]]Reduced risk of flooding or mechanical disturbance.

items.text: The larva may move across the plant or descend to the soil on a silken thread, then enter between soil particles or beneath surface residues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva may move across the plant or descend to the soil on a silken thread, then enter between soil particles or beneath surface residues.

items.text: Difference Between the Pupa and the Cocoon
The terms pupa and cocoon are commonly confused, although they do not have the same meaning.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The terms pupa and cocoon are commonly confused, although they do not have the same meaning.

items.text: Pupa
The pupa is the insect’s own body during transformation from larva to adult moth.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa is the insect’s own body during transformation from larva to adult moth.

items.text: Inside it, the following processes occur:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inside it, the following processes occur:

items: Breakdown and reorganization of larval tissues.
Formation of the adult reproductive system.
Completion of the moth’s body form.

items.text: Cocoon
The cocoon is the external silken covering spun by the larva around itself before transforming into a pupa.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cocoon is the external silken covering spun by the larva around itself before transforming into a pupa.

items.text: The cocoon may consist of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cocoon may consist of:

items.text: Therefore:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore:

items.text: > The pupa is the insect itself, whereas the cocoon is the covering surrounding it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]> The pupa is the insect itself, whereas the cocoon is the covering surrounding it.

items.text: The pupa is not always enclosed within a dense, clearly visible cocoon; the silken covering may be thin or nearly transparent, particularly when pupation occurs on leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa is not always enclosed within a dense, clearly visible cocoon; the silken covering may be thin or nearly transparent, particularly when pupation occurs on leaves.

items.text: General Appearance of the Pupa
The pupa of Tuta absoluta is of the type known as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa of Tuta absoluta is of the type known as:

items.text: Obtect Pupa
In this type, the developing adult appendages, such as the wings, legs, and antennae, are fixed close to the body surface and are not freely movable.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In this type, the developing adult appendages, such as the wings, legs, and antennae, are fixed close to the body surface and are not freely movable.

items.text: The pupa is characterized by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa is characterized by:

items: A narrower and more tapered posterior end.
Distinct wing cases on both sides of the thorax and abdomen.
Leg and antennal sheaths attached closely to the body.
A posterior structure that assists anchorage inside the cocoon.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A narrower and more tapered posterior end.
[[prepared]]Distinct wing cases on both sides of the thorax and abdomen.
[[prepared]]Leg and antennal sheaths attached closely to the body.
[[prepared]]A posterior structure that assists anchorage inside the cocoon.

items.text: The pupa remains motionless most of the time, but it may move the abdominal segments when touched or disturbed, particularly during the early part of the pupal period.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa remains motionless most of the time, but it may move the abdominal segments when touched or disturbed, particularly during the early part of the pupal period.

items.text: Parts of the Pupal Body
Although the external boundaries are less distinct than in the larva or moth, the main body regions can still be recognized.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Although the external boundaries are less distinct than in the larva or moth, the main body regions can still be recognized.

items.text: Head Region
The head is located at the anterior end, where the following can be observed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The head is located at the anterior end, where the following can be observed:

items: Positions of the developing compound eyes.
General outline of the head compressed against the thorax.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]General outline of the head compressed against the thorax.

items.text: Thorax
The thorax carries structures that will develop into:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The thorax carries structures that will develop into:

items: Muscles required for flight and movement.

items.text: The wing cases appear as long, smooth areas extending from the thorax toward the abdomen.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The wing cases appear as long, smooth areas extending from the thorax toward the abdomen.

items.text: Abdomen
The abdomen appears to consist of successive segments, with the posterior segments being more distinct and more mobile.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The abdomen appears to consist of successive segments, with the posterior segments being more distinct and more mobile.

items.text: Anchoring structures occur at the posterior end and help keep the pupa secured within the cocoon and prevent slipping as the moth moves during emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Anchoring structures occur at the posterior end and help keep the pupa secured within the cocoon and prevent slipping as the moth moves during emergence.

items.text: Pupal Size
The average length of a Tuta absoluta pupa is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The average length of a Tuta absoluta pupa is approximately:

items.text: In many field descriptions, the general length is close to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In many field descriptions, the general length is close to:

items.text: Approximately 5 mm.
Female pupae are larger than male pupae on average.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Female pupae are larger than male pupae on average.

items.text: Measurements of Male Pupae
Measurements of Female Pupae
These measurements alone should not be used to determine sex because large males may overlap in size with small females.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These measurements alone should not be used to determine sex because large males may overlap in size with small females.

items.text: Pupal dimensions are affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupal dimensions are affected by:

items: Larval exposure to insecticides or diseases.

items.text: Pupal Weight and Its Significance
Pupal weight reflects the quantity of nutritional resources stored by the larva before pupation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupal weight reflects the quantity of nutritional resources stored by the larva before pupation.

items.text: Pupae produced from well-fed larvae are usually:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupae produced from well-fed larvae are usually:

items: More capable of producing healthy moths.
More likely to produce highly fecund females.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]More likely to produce highly fecund females.

items.text: Small or low-weight pupae may result from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Small or low-weight pupae may result from:

items: Exposure to a sublethal insecticide dose.

items.text: Small pupal size does not necessarily mean mortality, but it may affect the characteristics, fecundity, and longevity of the resulting moth.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Small pupal size does not necessarily mean mortality, but it may affect the characteristics, fecundity, and longevity of the resulting moth.

items.text: Change in Pupal Color
Pupal color changes clearly as internal development progresses.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupal color changes clearly as internal development progresses.

During the first hours, the pupa is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]During the first hours, the pupa is:

items.text: At this stage, the outer covering has not yet hardened completely.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]At this stage, the outer covering has not yet hardened completely.

items.text: Mid-Pupal Period
The color gradually changes to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The color gradually changes to:

items.text: The covering becomes more sclerotized, and details of the wings and segments become clearer.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The covering becomes more sclerotized, and details of the wings and segments become clearer.

items.text: Near Adult Emergence
Before adult emergence, the pupa becomes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before adult emergence, the pupa becomes:

items.text: The dark eyes and wings of the developing moth become visible through the pupal covering.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The dark eyes and wings of the developing moth become visible through the pupal covering.

items.text: Gradual darkening before emergence is a normal change, but it differs from irregular blackening caused by death or fungal infection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Gradual darkening before emergence is a normal change, but it differs from irregular blackening caused by death or fungal infection.

items.text: Live and Dead Pupae
Indicators of a Live Pupa

items: The body remains full and is not shrunken.
No odor of decomposition is present.
A healthy moth emerges at the end of the developmental period.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The body remains full and is not shrunken.
[[prepared]]A healthy moth emerges at the end of the developmental period.

items.text: Indicators of a Dead Pupa

items: Fixed black coloration unrelated to moth development.
White, green, or dark fungal growth.
No moth emergence after the expected period has passed.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Fixed black coloration unrelated to moth development.
[[prepared]]No moth emergence after the expected period has passed.

items.text: Dark coloration alone is not sufficient to determine pupal death because a healthy pupa naturally becomes dark before adult emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dark coloration alone is not sufficient to determine pupal death because a healthy pupa naturally becomes dark before adult emergence.

items.text: Pupation Sites
Tuta absoluta can pupate in several locations. Selection of the pupation site depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta can pupate in several locations. Selection of the pupation site depends on:

items: Proximity of the larva to the soil.
Degree of disturbance to the plant.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Proximity of the larva to the soil.

items.text: Pupation sites include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupation sites include:

items: 10. In packages, crates, and packing materials.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]10. In packages, crates, and packing materials.

items.text: Pupation in Soil
A proportion of fully grown larvae leave the plant and descend to the soil surface, then enter the upper soil layer.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A proportion of fully grown larvae leave the plant and descend to the soil surface, then enter the upper soil layer.

items.text: Pupae are usually found:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupae are usually found:

items: Within approximately the upper 1–2 cm of soil.
Among sand particles and loose soil.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Within approximately the upper 1–2 cm of soil.

items.text: The larva spins a silken cocoon to which soil particles may adhere, making its color resemble the surrounding medium and rendering detection difficult.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva spins a silken cocoon to which soil particles may adhere, making its color resemble the surrounding medium and rendering detection difficult.

items.text: Pupation in soil increases when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupation in soil increases when:

items: The crop approaches the end of the season.
Soil is loose and moderately moist.
Plants are disturbed by movement or harvesting.
Larvae descend from the canopy on silken threads.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The crop approaches the end of the season.
[[prepared]]Plants are disturbed by movement or harvesting.
[[prepared]]Larvae descend from the canopy on silken threads.

items.text: Pupation on the Plant
Larvae can pupate on the canopy inside a thin silken cocoon.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae can pupate on the canopy inside a thin silken cocoon.

items.text: Common sites include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Common sites include:

items: A fold along the leaf margin.
Beneath sepals or in other protected sites.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Beneath sepals or in other protected sites.

items.text: The larva spins silken threads that close the space or secure the leaf margins around its body.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva spins silken threads that close the space or secure the leaf margins around its body.

items.text: The cocoon may be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cocoon may be:

items: Mixed with frass or plant fragments.
Difficult to see without lifting the leaf or opening the fold.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Difficult to see without lifting the leaf or opening the fold.

items.text: Pupation in Processing and Fresh-Market Tomatoes
The distribution of pupation sites varies according to the production system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The distribution of pupation sites varies according to the production system.

items.text: In some processing tomato fields, soil becomes more important as a pupation site because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In some processing tomato fields, soil becomes more important as a pupation site because of:

items: Branches being close to the soil surface.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Branches being close to the soil surface.

items.text: In some fresh-market and greenhouse crops, a greater proportion of pupae may be found:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In some fresh-market and greenhouse crops, a greater proportion of pupae may be found:

items.text: There is no single fixed pupation site for all production systems. Some field studies recorded higher numbers on leaves than in soil, whereas other observations showed a predominance of ground pupation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]There is no single fixed pupation site for all production systems. Some field studies recorded higher numbers on leaves than in soil, whereas other observations showed a predominance of ground pupation.

items.text: Pupation in Containers and Handling Areas
Pupation is not restricted to the field. Larvae may leave fruits or accompanying leaves during:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupation is not restricted to the field. Larvae may leave fruits or accompanying leaves during:

items: Arrival of fruits at the market or processing plant.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Arrival of fruits at the market or processing plant.

items.text: They may then pupate in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may then pupate in:

items.text: This characteristic allows the pest to move into new locations because the adult moth may emerge after the package has reached an area far from the original field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This characteristic allows the pest to move into new locations because the adult moth may emerge after the package has reached an area far from the original field.

items.text: Cocoon Formation
The larva produces silken threads from the silk glands, then moves its head and body to construct a covering around itself.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva produces silken threads from the silk glands, then moves its head and body to construct a covering around itself.

items.text: The process proceeds through the following stages:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The process proceeds through the following stages:

items: 2. Attachment of initial threads to the substrate.
4. Incorporation of leaf fragments or soil particles.
8. Appearance of the pupa inside the cocoon.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]2. Attachment of initial threads to the substrate.
[[prepared]]4. Incorporation of leaf fragments or soil particles.
[[prepared]]8. Appearance of the pupa inside the cocoon.

items.text: The cocoon may be relatively strong when pupation occurs in soil, where silk becomes mixed with sand particles, whereas it is thinner when pupation occurs on leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cocoon may be relatively strong when pupation occurs in soil, where silk becomes mixed with sand particles, whereas it is thinner when pupation occurs on leaves.

items.text: Duration of the Pupal Stage
The duration of the pupal stage depends primarily on temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The duration of the pupal stage depends primarily on temperature.

items.text: Under moderate and warm conditions, the pupal stage generally lasts approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under moderate and warm conditions, the pupal stage generally lasts approximately:

items.text: 6–10 days.
However, it may extend for several weeks under cold conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, it may extend for several weeks under cold conditions.

items.text: Studies conducted under constant temperatures recorded the following values:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies conducted under constant temperatures recorded the following values:

headers: Mean Duration of the Pupal Stage

items.text: These values demonstrate a clear inverse relationship between temperature and pupal duration:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These values demonstrate a clear inverse relationship between temperature and pupal duration:

items.text: > The higher the temperature within the suitable range, the shorter the period required for transformation into the adult moth.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]> The higher the temperature within the suitable range, the shorter the period required for transformation into the adult moth.

items.text: However, a shorter duration does not always mean that the highest temperature is optimal, because extreme heat may reduce survival or the quality of emerging moths.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, a shorter duration does not always mean that the highest temperature is optimal, because extreme heat may reduce survival or the quality of emerging moths.

items.text: Cold Tolerance
The pupa is one of the important stages for pest survival during cold periods, particularly when protected within soil, crop residues, or greenhouses.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa is one of the important stages for pest survival during cold periods, particularly when protected within soil, crop residues, or greenhouses.

items.text: A study of continuous cold exposure showed that pupae were able to survive at:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A study of continuous cold exposure showed that pupae were able to survive at:

items.text: 5°C for 30 or 60 days
And subsequently produced moths after being returned to suitable conditions, although emergence declined as exposure duration increased.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And subsequently produced moths after being returned to suitable conditions, although emergence declined as exposure duration increased.

items.text: After continuous exposure for 90 days, some pupae appeared to remain alive, but no complete adult moths emerged.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After continuous exposure for 90 days, some pupae appeared to remain alive, but no complete adult moths emerged.

items.text: Under a gradual cooling program:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Under a gradual cooling program:

items: Approximately 47% of moths emerged after a period at 11°C.
Emergence declined to approximately 12% during the following period at 10°C.
No emergence occurred when temperature was subsequently lowered to 8°C.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Approximately 47% of moths emerged after a period at 11°C.
[[prepared]]Emergence declined to approximately 12% during the following period at 10°C.
[[prepared]]No emergence occurred when temperature was subsequently lowered to 8°C.

items.text: These results show that pupae can tolerate extended cold exposure, but not indefinitely, and their ability to complete development declines as the duration and intensity of cold increase.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These results show that pupae can tolerate extended cold exposure, but not indefinitely, and their ability to complete development declines as the duration and intensity of cold increase.

items.text: Effect of Soil Moisture
Pupae require moderate moisture to prevent desiccation, but they are damaged by excessive water saturation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupae require moderate moisture to prevent desiccation, but they are damaged by excessive water saturation.

items.text: Soil moisture affects:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Soil moisture affects:

items: Adult emergence to the soil surface.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The results indicate that:

items: Moderate moisture provides the highest survival.
Saturation or high moisture reduces survival and emergence.
Moderately moist soil provides the best balance for pupae.

[[prepared]]Moderately moist soil provides the best balance for pupae.

items.text: These percentages are not fixed for all soil types; results vary according to soil texture, temperature, aeration, salinity, and pupal depth.

The pupa requires oxygen through its spiracles. Therefore, continuous waterlogging or soil saturation leads to:

items.text: Effect of Desiccation
Severe desiccation causes water loss from the pupa, particularly when it occurs:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Severe desiccation causes water loss from the pupa, particularly when it occurs:

items: In packages exposed to air and heat.

items: Emergence of a deformed or weak moth.

items.text: The cocoon and surface soil provide partial protection from desiccation, but they do not completely prevent water loss.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The cocoon and surface soil provide partial protection from desiccation, but they do not completely prevent water loss.

items.text: Effect of High Temperature
Pupal duration becomes shorter as temperature rises to 30°C within the tolerable range, but prolonged high temperature or temperatures above the upper threshold cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupal duration becomes shorter as temperature rises to 30°C within the tolerable range, but prolonged high temperature or temperatures above the upper threshold cause:

items: Failure of transformation into the adult.

items.text: Pupae inside soil are less exposed to temperature fluctuations than pupae located on leaves or exposed containers.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupae inside soil are less exposed to temperature fluctuations than pupae located on leaves or exposed containers.

The empty pupal case remains at the pupation site after adult emergence.

items.text: Distinguishing Male and Female Pupae
Pupal sex can be determined by examining the ventral surface under a stereomicroscope.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupal sex can be determined by examining the ventral surface under a stereomicroscope.

items.text: Female Pupa
It is characterized by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is characterized by:

items: A longitudinal genital opening or slit.
Located in the middle of the eighth abdominal segment.
Larger size and weight on average.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Located in the middle of the eighth abdominal segment.

items.text: Male Pupa
It is characterized by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is characterized by:

items: The genital opening being located on the ninth abdominal segment.
Absence of the female slit on the eighth segment.
A more slender body on average.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The genital opening being located on the ninth abdominal segment.
[[prepared]]Absence of the female slit on the eighth segment.

items.text: Sex determination is used in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Sex determination is used in:

items: Evaluation of insecticide effects on each sex.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Evaluation of insecticide effects on each sex.

items.text: In routine field inspection, determining the sex of every pupa is not necessary.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In routine field inspection, determining the sex of every pupa is not necessary.

items.text: Pupal Sensitivity to Insecticides
The pupa is a non-feeding stage and therefore is not affected by insecticides that require ingestion in the same manner as larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa is a non-feeding stage and therefore is not affected by insecticides that require ingestion in the same manner as larvae.

items.text: The efficacy of many foliar sprays against pupae is also reduced because pupae:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The efficacy of many foliar sprays against pupae is also reduced because pupae:

items: Do not feed on treated tissue.

items.text: Some materials may act through contact, fumigation, or penetration, but success depends on direct delivery of the material to the pupa.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some materials may act through contact, fumigation, or penetration, but success depends on direct delivery of the material to the pupa.

items.text: A spray that is effective against larvae should not automatically be considered effective against pupae present in the soil.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A spray that is effective against larvae should not automatically be considered effective against pupae present in the soil.

items.text: However, a pupa enclosed within a cocoon and located inside soil is relatively protected.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, a pupa enclosed within a cocoon and located inside soil is relatively protected.

items.text: The efficacy of biological agents is affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The efficacy of biological agents is affected by:

items.text: Some experiments have shown that infection of Tuta pupae by entomopathogenic nematodes is possible, but mortality rates may be lower than those against larvae. Therefore, a management program should not rely on this method alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some experiments have shown that infection of Tuta pupae by entomopathogenic nematodes is possible, but mortality rates may be lower than those against larvae. Therefore, a management program should not rely on this method alone.

items.text: Importance of the Pupal Stage in Infestation Persistence
Pupae may remain present after:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupae may remain present after:

items: Part of the crop has been removed.
Produce has been transported from the field.

[[prepared]]Produce has been transported from the field.

items.text: Adult moths may then emerge and move to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Adult moths may then emerge and move to:

items.text: This explains the continued appearance of moths after fruits have been removed or after visible larval numbers on plants have declined.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This explains the continued appearance of moths after fruits have been removed or after visible larval numbers on plants have declined.

items.text: Importance of the Pupal Stage in Integrated Pest Management
Disposal of Crop Residues
Crop residues may contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Crop residues may contain:

items.text: Leaving these residues allows a new generation of moths to emerge.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaving these residues allows a new generation of moths to emerge.

items.text: Agricultural sanitation therefore includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Agricultural sanitation therefore includes:

items: Shredding plants immediately after harvest ends.
Avoiding the accumulation of plant piles near the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Avoiding the accumulation of plant piles near the field.

items.text: Tillage
Tillage after crop removal contributes to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tillage after crop removal contributes to:

items: Exposing some pupae to sunlight and desiccation.
Moving pupae to depths that impede moth emergence.
Exposing them to birds and predators.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Exposing some pupae to sunlight and desiccation.
[[prepared]]Moving pupae to depths that impede moth emergence.

items.text: However, tillage efficacy varies with depth, soil type, and moisture, and shallow tillage alone does not guarantee complete pupal control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, tillage efficacy varies with depth, soil type, and moisture, and shallow tillage alone does not guarantee complete pupal control.

items.text: Soil Solarization
Soil solarization during suitable seasons helps raise the temperature of the upper soil layer, affecting pupae and other stages beneath the cover.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Soil solarization during suitable seasons helps raise the temperature of the upper soil layer, affecting pupae and other stages beneath the cover.

Cleaning containers and handling areas prevents movement of:

items.text: Leaf, calyx, and damaged-fruit residues should be removed daily, and contaminated crates should not be returned to farms without cleaning.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Leaf, calyx, and damaged-fruit residues should be removed daily, and contaminated crates should not be returned to farms without cleaning.

items.text: Soil-Moisture Management
Irrigation should not be increased indiscriminately in an attempt to kill pupae because this may damage roots, increase diseases, and waste water.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Irrigation should not be increased indiscriminately in an attempt to kill pupae because this may damage roots, increase diseases, and waste water.

items.text: However, understanding pupal sensitivity to severe desiccation and saturation helps explain changes in pupal numbers under different irrigation systems and soil textures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, understanding pupal sensitivity to severe desiccation and saturation helps explain changes in pupal numbers under different irrigation systems and soil textures.

items.text: Summary of the Pupal Stage

The pupa is the non-feeding stage within which the larva of Tuta absoluta transforms into an adult moth. It is elongated or spindle-shaped and measures approximately 4.3–4.7 mm in length, with female pupae being larger than male pupae on average.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupa is the non-feeding stage within which the larva of Tuta absoluta transforms into an adult moth. It is elongated or spindle-shaped and measures approximately 4.3–4.7 mm in length, with female pupae being larger than male pupae on average.

items.text: A newly formed pupa is green or yellowish green, then turns brown and becomes dark before adult emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A newly formed pupa is green or yellowish green, then turns brown and becomes dark before adult emergence.

items.text: The insect pupates in soil, on leaves, inside folded leaves, within mines and crop residues, and in containers. Soil pupae are commonly located in the upper soil layer inside a silken cocoon to which sand particles adhere.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The insect pupates in soil, on leaves, inside folded leaves, within mines and crop residues, and in containers. Soil pupae are commonly located in the upper soil layer inside a silken cocoon to which sand particles adhere.

items.text: Pupal duration is affected by temperature. It is approximately 36.8 days at 10°C, declines to 10.2 days at 20°C, 8.4 days at 25°C, and 5.4 days at 30°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupal duration is affected by temperature. It is approximately 36.8 days at 10°C, declines to 10.2 days at 20°C, 8.4 days at 25°C, and 5.4 days at 30°C.

items.text: A range of 20–25°C provides suitable conditions for pupal development and adult emergence, while pupae can tolerate extended cold periods, especially in protected sites.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A range of 20–25°C provides suitable conditions for pupal development and adult emergence, while pupae can tolerate extended cold periods, especially in protected sites.

items.text: Pupae require moderate moisture. Both severe desiccation and water saturation reduce survival and emergence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupae require moderate moisture. Both severe desiccation and water saturation reduce survival and emergence.

items.text: Most foliar spray treatments do not reach pupae located inside soil or cocoons. Their management therefore depends mainly on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Most foliar spray treatments do not reach pupae located inside soil or cocoons. Their management therefore depends mainly on:

items: Shredding and tilling fields after harvest.
Use of appropriate biological agents within an integrated program.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Use of appropriate biological agents within an integrated program.

items.text: The pupal stage causes no direct damage, but neglecting it allows new adult moths to emerge and initiate a new infestation cycle in the same field or in neighboring crops.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pupal stage causes no direct damage, but neglecting it allows new adult moths to emerge and initiate a new infestation cycle in the same field or in neighboring crops.
